infobox.key: This article is part of [the series](/wiki/Category:Cantonese_language "Category:Cantonese language") on the

infobox.value: | * [Nasal-stop alternation](/wiki/Cantonese_nasal-stop_alternation "Cantonese nasal-stop alternation") * [Changed tone](/wiki/Changed_tone "Changed tone") * [Proper Cantonese pronunciation](/wiki/Proper_Cantonese_pronunciation "Proper Cantonese pronunciation") * [IPA](/wiki/Help:IPA_for_Cantonese "Help:IPA for Cantonese")

was developed by [Yale](/wiki/Yale_University "Yale University") scholar [Gerard P. Kok](/w/index.php?title=Gerard_P._Kok&action=edit&redlink=1 "Gerard P. Kok (page does not exist)") for his and Parker Po-fei Huang's textbook *Speak Cantonese* initially circulated in looseleaf form in 1952[[1]](#cite_note-1) but later published in 1958.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Unlike the [Yale romanization of Mandarin](/wiki/Yale_romanization_of_Mandarin "Yale romanization of Mandarin"), it is still widely used in books and dictionaries, especially for foreign learners of [Cantonese](/wiki/Cantonese "Cantonese"). It shares some similarities with [Hanyu Pinyin](/wiki/Hanyu_Pinyin "Hanyu Pinyin") in that unvoiced, unaspirated [consonants](/wiki/Consonants "Consonants") are represented by letters traditionally used in English and most other European languages to represent voiced sounds. For example, [p] is represented as *b* in Yale, whereas its aspirated counterpart, [pʰ] is represented as *p*.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Students attending the [Chinese University of Hong Kong](/wiki/Chinese_University_of_Hong_Kong "Chinese University of Hong Kong")'s New-Asia Yale-in-China Chinese Language Center are taught using Yale romanization.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

outgoing_links.text: writing Cantonese as an alphabetic language

| * [Fanchit](/wiki/Fanqie "Fanqie") * [Braille](/wiki/Cantonese_Braille "Cantonese Braille") * [Bopomofo](/wiki/Cantonese_Bopomofo "Cantonese Bopomofo") * + [Pinyin](/wiki/Cantonese_pinyin "Cantonese pinyin")   + Yale   + [Jyutping](/wiki/Jyutping "Jyutping") |
| [Phonology](/wiki/Cantonese_phonology "Cantonese phonology") |
| * [Nasal-stop alternation](/wiki/Cantonese_nasal-stop_alternation "Cantonese nasal-stop alternation") * [Changed tone](/wiki/Changed_tone "Changed tone") * [Proper Cantonese pronunciation](/wiki/Proper_Cantonese_pronunciation "Proper Cantonese pronunciation") * [IPA](/wiki/Help:IPA_for_Cantonese "Help:IPA for Cantonese") |
| * [v](/wiki/Template:Cantonese_language "Template:Cantonese language") * [t](/wiki/Template_talk:Cantonese_language "Template talk:Cantonese language") * [e](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Template:Cantonese_language "Special:EditPage/Template:Cantonese language") |

This article contains **[phonetic transcriptions](/wiki/Phonetic_transcription "Phonetic transcription") in the [International Phonetic Alphabet](/wiki/International_Phonetic_Alphabet "International Phonetic Alphabet") (IPA)**. For an introductory guide on IPA symbols, see [Help:IPA](/wiki/Help:IPA "Help:IPA"). For the distinction between [ ], / / and ⟨ ⟩, see [IPA § Brackets and transcription delimiters](/wiki/International_Phonetic_Alphabet#Brackets_and_transcription_delimiters "International Phonetic Alphabet").

The **Yale romanization of Cantonese** was developed by [Yale](/wiki/Yale_University "Yale University") scholar [Gerard P. Kok](/w/index.php?title=Gerard_P._Kok&action=edit&redlink=1 "Gerard P. Kok (page does not exist)") for his and Parker Po-fei Huang's textbook *Speak Cantonese* initially circulated in looseleaf form in 1952[[1]](#cite_note-1) but later published in 1958.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Unlike the [Yale romanization of Mandarin](/wiki/Yale_romanization_of_Mandarin "Yale romanization of Mandarin"), it is still widely used in books and dictionaries, especially for foreign learners of [Cantonese](/wiki/Cantonese "Cantonese"). It shares some similarities with [Hanyu Pinyin](/wiki/Hanyu_Pinyin "Hanyu Pinyin") in that unvoiced, unaspirated [consonants](/wiki/Consonants "Consonants") are represented by letters traditionally used in English and most other European languages to represent voiced sounds. For example, [p] is represented as *b* in Yale, whereas its aspirated counterpart, [pʰ] is represented as *p*.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Students attending the [Chinese University of Hong Kong](/wiki/Chinese_University_of_Hong_Kong "Chinese University of Hong Kong")'s New-Asia Yale-in-China Chinese Language Center are taught using Yale romanization.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Some enthusiasts employ Yale romanisation to explore [writing Cantonese as an alphabetic language](/wiki/Romanization_of_Cantonese "Romanization of Cantonese") [[zh](https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E7%B2%B5%E6%96%87%E6%8B%89%E4%B8%81%E5%8C%96 "zh:粵文拉丁化")].[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

* Only the finals *m* and *ng* can be used as standalone [nasal syllables](/wiki/Syllabic_nasal "Syllabic nasal").

## Tones

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yale_romanization_of_Cantonese&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Tones")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/Cantonese_Tones.png/250px-Cantonese_Tones.png)](/wiki/File:Cantonese_Tones.png)

Graphical representation of the tones of six-tone Cantonese.

Modern Cantonese has up to seven phonemic tones. Cantonese Yale represents these tones using a combination of diacritics and the letter *h*.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) Traditional Chinese linguistics treats the tones in syllables ending with a stop consonant as separate "[entering tones](/wiki/Entering_tone "Entering tone")". Cantonese Yale follows modern linguistic conventions in treating these the same as the high-flat, mid-flat and low-flat tones, respectively.

| No. | Description | IPA & [Chao](/wiki/Tone_letter#Chao_tone_letters_(IPA) "Tone letter")  tone numbers | Yale representation | | |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | high-flat | ˥ 55 | sī | sīn | sīk |
| high-falling | ˥˨ 52 | sì | sìn |  |
| 2 | mid-rising | ˧˥ 35 | sí | sín |  |
| 3 | mid-flat | ˧ 33 | si | sin | sik |
| 4 | low-falling | ˨˩ 21 | sìh | sìhn |  |
| 5 | low-rising | ˨˧ 23 | síh | síhn |  |
| 6 | low-flat | ˨ 22 | sih | sihn | sihk |

## Examples

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yale_romanization_of_Cantonese&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Examples")]

| [Traditional](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_character "Traditional Chinese character") | [Simplified](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_character "Simplified Chinese character") | Romanization |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 廣州話 | 广州话 | gwóng jāu wá |
| 粵語 | 粤语 | yuht yúh |
| 你好 | | néih hóu |

Sample transcription of one of the [*300 Tang Poems*](/wiki/Three_Hundred_Tang_Poems "Three Hundred Tang Poems") by [Meng Haoran](/wiki/Meng_Haoran "Meng Haoran"):

| 春曉 [孟浩然](/wiki/Meng_Haoran "Meng Haoran") | Chèun híu Maahng Houh-yìhn |
| --- | --- |
| 春眠不覺曉， | *Chèun mìhn bāt gok híu,* |
| 處處聞啼鳥。 | *chyu chyu màhn tàih níuh.* |
| 夜來風雨聲， | *Yeh lòih fùng yúh sìng,* |
| 花落知多少？ | *fà lohk jì dò síu?* |

Note concerning the jì in the last line of the poem that it is pronounced as high flat here because immediately followed by a tone that begins high and yet that this Romanization's conventions mark it nonetheless as high falling, and the user then needs to remember this rule of tone-sandhi. (Interested readers can confirm this convention by looking at for instance the dictionary by Kwan Choi Wah among the works in the list at bottom below.)
